coredata.dc:title: Toward near-white electroluminescence with enhanced blue emission from carbon dots in PEDOT:PSS/ZnO organic/inorganic hybrid heterojunctions
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Chengjun
Zhou, Yulu
Tao, Xiaoma
Chen, Hongmei
Ouyang, Yifang
Mo, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) produced by using carbon dots (CDs) and related materials are of great interest for their huge potential in backlight and solid-state lighting applications. Besides the tremendous efforts to use CDs as phosphors of GaN-based blue or UV LED chips to produce photoluminescent WLEDs, it is really not easy to produce electroluminescent WLEDs with CDs as the active layer owing to the complex emission mechanism of the CDs. Here, by combining the merits of both the blue emissive CDs and ZnO nanowires (NWs), near-white electroluminescence (EL) is demonstrated by enhancing blue emission from the blue CDs and with the help of a thin PMMA polymer layer for the PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs organic/inorganic hybrid heterojunction LEDs. By interfacial modification with the thin PMMA layer, EL intensity of the device is boosted strikingly (especially for the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs), making the emission color of the device tune from yellowish green toward near white. The findings in this work may pave a feasible way to realize CDs-based electroluminescent WLEDs and give a practical demonstration to regulate the blue emission of ZnO to produce WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZnO nanowire
Organic/inorganic
Electroluminescence
Light-emitting diode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of preparation of the CDs and the OIHH LEDs. Top-right inset shows the TEM image of the CDs and photographs of the CDs in DMF under visible (left) and 365 nm UV (right) illumination.

(a)–(d) Surface images of ZnO NWs, CDs/ZnO NWs, PMMA/CDs/ZnO NWs, and PEDOT:PSS/PMMA/CDs/ZnO NWs, respectively. (e) Cross-sectional image of the hybrid heterojunction. (f) XRD pattern of the CDs/ZnO NWs on ITO substrate. The inset shows the zoomed-in XRD pattern (2θ from 10 to 40°), with pure CDs as reference. The black squares indicate the XRD signals of ITO.
(a) PL spectra excited by a 325 nm He–Cd laser. (b) I–V curves of LED#1, LED#2, and LED#3.
(a–b), (c–d), and (e–f) EL spectra and their corresponding peak deconvolutions with Gaussian functions of LED#1, LED#2, and LED#3, respectively.

(a) Fitting peak intensity and (b) enhancement factor versus Peak 1 to 7, as concluded from the peak-deconvolution results. (c) CIE (1931) plots of the devices. (d) Energy band alignment diagram of the device components of LED#3.

head.ce:title: Toward near-white electroluminescence with enhanced blue emission from carbon dots in PEDOT:PSS/ZnO organic/inorganic hybrid heterojunctions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center on Nanoenergy Research, School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Center on Nanoenergy Research, School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) produced by using carbon dots (CDs) and related materials are of great interest for their huge potential in backlight and solid-state lighting applications. Besides the tremendous efforts to use CDs as phosphors of GaN-based blue or UV LED chips to produce photoluminescent WLEDs, it is really not easy to produce electroluminescent WLEDs with CDs as the active layer owing to the complex emission mechanism of the CDs. Here, by combining the merits of both the blue emissive CDs and ZnO nanowires (NWs), near-white electroluminescence (EL) is demonstrated by enhancing blue emission from the blue CDs and with the help of a thin PMMA polymer layer for the PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs organic/inorganic hybrid heterojunction LEDs. By interfacial modification with the thin PMMA layer, EL intensity of the device is boosted strikingly (especially for the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs), making the emission color of the device tune from yellowish green toward near white. The findings in this work may pave a feasible way to realize CDs-based electroluminescent WLEDs and give a practical demonstration to regulate the blue emission of ZnO to produce WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have attracted extensive attention in past years owing to their tremendous potential in backlight for display industry and next-generation solid-state lighting sources [1–3]. There have been several strategies proposed to realize WLEDs. An all-inorganic strategy by using yellow YAG:Ce phosphors excited by a GaN-based blue LED chip has been one of the most successful way to obtain WLEDs [4]. However, in this type of WLED, owing to the passive luminescence of the yellow phosphors, the red spectral signal is relatively weak and the blue emission from the GaN-based LED chip usually becomes dominant at higher injection currents, resulting in low color rendering index (CRI) and high color temperature of the WLED toward blue colour gamut [5]. Another strategy is to produce all-organic WLEDs by mixing the red, green and blue (RGB) emissions from different organic semiconductors [6]. This method can provide flexible white-light sources with high brightness and CRI by any required dimensions, but the complexity of the techniques during the fabrication and the long-term stability of devices are still major concerns. In recent years, following the emergence and fast development of fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) with ultra-high quantum yields (QYs), organic/inorganic hybrid heterostructures (OIHHs) have also been applied to produce WLEDs owing to the merits of both comparable high stability like the all-inorganic WLEDs and desirable flexibility like the all-organic WLEDs by using the OIHHs [7–9].

Quantum-sized carbon dots (CDs), consisting of the internal carbon core and rich external functional groups, have attracted numerous attentions in producing optoelectronic devices due to their excellent photophysical properties (such as low toxicity, high biocompatibility, simplicity in synthesis and high QYs) [10–14]. Therefore, it is not surprising to use CDs to produce WLEDs. Up to date, the easiest strategy with lowest risk to produce CDs-based WLEDs has been use of CDs as phosphors of GaN-based blue (or UV) LED chips, the similar way as the successful YAG:Ce phosphors. Following this method, much inspiring work has been demonstrated. Sun et al. used down-conversion CDs layer to obtain WLEDs by adjusting thickness of the CDs layer and concentration of the CDs in the polymer matrix [15]. Luo et al. used white-light-emitting graphene QDs as phosphors of UV emissive 4,4-bis(carbazol-9-yl)biphenyl (CBP) layer to obtain WLED with luminance and external quantum efficiency (EQE) over 200 cd/m2 and 0.2%, respectively [16]. In addition, blue emissive CDs with green and red emissive semiconductor QDs (like ZCIS QDs [17]) or polymer dots [5] were also mixed in the polymer matrices as white-light phosphors to produce WLEDs. Though enormous strides have been made in this type of CDs-based WLEDs, the obvious excitation dependent emission of the CDs (related to the surface molecule or defect states [18]) in most of the WLEDs has limited their further development because one has to use different excitation source in one single device for the purpose of obtaining high-efficiency emission, especially for the longer wavelength (yellow or red) emissions. To address the issue, WLEDs as well as multicolor LEDs using the excitation independent CDs have been developed and significant progress has been achieved in recent years [19–21].

In spite of the big achievements in the WLEDs with CDs as phosphors, it should be noted that this type of CDs-based WLEDs fundamentally belongs to photoluminescent devices and highly relies on the quality of the GaN-based LED chip (rather than the CDs). Hence, they are less important than developing the real electroluminescent WLEDs by using CDs as the active emission layer. Actually, several previous reports have shown the great potential to use CDs as the active layer to produce electroluminescent WLEDs. Wang et al. [22] and Yuan et al. [23] sandwiched CDs and green CDs-blended poly(N-vinyl carbazole) (PVK) between poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene-sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) hole transport layer (HTL) and 1,3,5-tris(N-phenylbenzimidazol-2-yl) benzene (TPBI) electron injection layer (EIL), respectively, to produce WLEDs with luminance up to 2050 cd/m2. Zhang et al. [24] and Son et al. [25] used poly-(N,N′-bis(4-butylphenyl)-N,N′-bis(phenyl) benzidine) (poly-TPD) as the HTL and ZnO nanoparticles as the EIL to form a OIHH WLEDs to yield luminance as high as 798 cd/m2. These reports gave good demonstrations to use CDs as the active emitter to produce electroluminescent WLEDs, but owing to the interference of the EL from the organic HTL (poly-TPD) or EIL (TPBI), a clear underlying EL mechanism for the CDs has not been directly demonstrated yet.

In principle, to produce electroluminescent WLEDs based on CDs, one needs three basic RGB emissions from the active materials of the device. At present, since most of the reported fluorescent CDs with high efficiencies emit blue light, it is quite rational to use the blue CDs as the blue light source for the WLEDs. Besides the blue emission, however, one has to introduce additional green and red emissions (which may be generated from green and red emissive CDs or other semiconductors) to the active layer of the device. Among the numerous semiconductors, zinc oxide (ZnO) having direct bandgap and large exciton binding energy of 60 meV [26] is a promising candidate because ZnO itself can emit the green to red visible emissions either in photoluminescence (PL) or EL due to the naturally intrinsic defects in ZnO, especially for solution-grown ZnO nanowires (NWs) or nanocrystals (NCs). Although the green to red emissions in ZnO were frequently treated as drawbacks previously, if one can make good use of these defect-related emissions, WLEDs may also be achieved simply by . In fact, it has been demonstrated in our previous work that one can obtain near-white-light LEDs by utilizing and regulating the intrinsic defect-related green emission of the ZnO NCs [27]. The authors also revealed that the blue emission in the ZnO-based near-white LEDs was too insufficient to further tune the color of the LED toward ideal white. In this regard, WLEDs would be available with the help of the blue emissive CDs by the superposition of blue light from CDs and green to red emissions from ZnO.

In this work, we demonstrate near-white-light EL by enhancing the blue emission from blue emissive CDs and with the help of a thin poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) polymer layer for the PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs OIHH LEDs. Addition of the blue emissive CDs substantially improves the blue light that is insufficient for the PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs OIHH LEDs to produce white-light emission. Interfacial modification with the thin PMMA layer between the CDs and HTL of PEDOT:PSS further boosts the blue emission from the CDs as well as the ZnO NWs, remarkably making the emission color of the device tune from yellowish green toward near white. Our work paves a feasible way to realize WLEDs by using CDs as the emissive layer and also gives a practical demonstration to regulate the blue emission of ZnO to produce WLEDs. The emission mechanism of the CDs and function of the thin PMMA layer are analyzed and discussed in this work.

Schematic preparation of the CDs and the OIHH LEDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The blue-emissive CDs were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and urea by hydrothermal method as described elsewhere [28]. Brown CDs powders were obtained by collecting and drying the dialyzed solution in the dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off of 3500 D) at 80 °C under vacuum for use. ZnO NWs were fabricated via a low-temperature solution method on precleaned indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrates (with sheet resistance of ~7 Ω/□, transmittance of ~84% and size of 10 × 12 × 1.1 mm) by using Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and hexamethylenetetramine (HMT, C6H12N4) as precursors. Prior to growth of the ZnO NWs, a ZnO thin film with thickness of ~250 nm was deposited on the ITO substrates as the seed layer in a radio-frequency (RF) magnetron sputtering system using a ZnO ceramic target (99.99% purity). The ZnO seed layer was annealed at 400 °C for 40 min in air to improve the crystallinity. ZnO NWs were produced on the seeded ITO glass substrates in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 80 °C for 8 h, using equi-molar Zn(NO3)2 and HMT (0.01 M) in 200 mL deionized (DI) water as the nutrient solution. After the reaction was finished, the samples were thoroughly rinsed with DI water to remove the residues and then dried under vacuum at 80 °C for use.

For preparation of the hybrid LEDs, the CDs powders were firstly dissolved in anhydrous dimethylformamide (DMF) with a concentration of 2 mg/mL. The CDs solution was then stirred for 24 h and filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane for use. The CDs were deposited onto the ZnO NWs by dropwise casting and drying at 80 °C for 10 min. This process was repeated for 6 times to ensure the complete coverage of the CDs on the surface of the ZnO NWs. Afterward, PMMA dissolved in acetone (1 wt%) was spin-casted onto the CDs-coated ZnO NWs under 2000 rpm for 30 s to yield a thin PMMA film on the CDs/ZnO NWs after being repeated twice and baked at 80 °C for 5 min. PEDOT:PSS was spin-coated onto the PMMA layer under 3000 rpm for 30 s to form the OIHH after being annealed at 80 °C for 30 min. Finally, silver (Ag) electrodes were sputtered on the PEDOT:PSS as anode by DC magnetron sputtering with an optimized power of 20 W for 5 min under an argon flow of 20 sccm. ITO was used as the cathode directly. Three types of hybrid LEDs were investigated as comparisons; that is, LED#1: Ag/PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs/ITO, LED#2: Ag/PEDOT: PSS/CDs/ZnO NWs/ITO, and LED#3: Ag/PEDOT:PSS/PMMA/CDs/ZnO NWs/ITO.

Morphologies of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in Hitachi SU8220. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed in JEOL JEM-2100. The crystal structures of the CDs and CDs/ZnO NWs were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Tongda TD-3500, Cu Kα radiation). Current-voltage (I–V) characteristics were measured by a Keithley 2400 sourcemeter. PL spectra were obtained by using a 325 nm He–Cd laser as the excitation source (IK3201R–F, Kimmon Koha) and a Zolix monochromator combining with a CCD detector (Andor iDus DU401A-BVF) to collect the emissions. EL spectra of the LEDs were collected by the same Zolix monochromator that was coupled with a photo-multiplier tube (PMT) to detect the light signals. The photographs of the CDs in DMF were taken via a Canon EOS 70D digital camera.

As shown in the TEM image in the top-right inset of Fig. 1, the as-produced blue CDs are sphere-like with an average size of ~3 nm and shine a distinct blue light under illumination of 365 nm UV light. Top-view SEM images in Fig. 2 (a)-(d) show the evolution of the surface morphologies of the samples in detail. Combining Fig. 2(a) with the cross-sectional image in Fig. 2(e), one can find that the ZnO NWs are vertically grown on the ITO glass substrate with an average length of 1.4 μm and gradually tapering shape from bottom to top. This morphology is quite familiar in solution-grown ZnO NWs and benefits the incorporation of the CDs [29,30]. After dropwise coating the blue CDs, voids among the ZnO NWs shown in Fig. 2(a) are perfectly filled with CDs, making the tapering ZnO NW tips obviously project from the CDs beneath (see Fig. 2(b)). However, the projecting tips of the ZnO NWs are not favorable for further constructing the heterojunction due to the rough surface induced by the incomplete coverage of the projecting tips. Insertion of a thin PMMA layer can solve this problem and yield a flat and uniform surface for further preparing the HTL of PEDOT:PSS, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Owing to the thin PMMA interlayer, dense and smooth PEDOT:PSS film without any cracks is produced (see Fig. 2(d)–(e)), providing a suitable flat surface for depositing the Ag anodes with good electrical contacts. The XRD pattern of the CDs/ZnO NWs on the ITO substrate in Fig. 2(f) shows a sharp and strong diffraction peak at 34.7° that corresponds to the (002) plane of wurtzite ZnO, indicating that the ZnO NWs are grown with a preferred direction perpendicular to the ITO substrate (consistent with the cross-sectional image in Fig. 2(e)). The broad peak centered at ~24° in both the XRD patterns of the pure CDs and CDs/ZnO NWs, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 2(f), is attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [31], proving the successful deposition of the blue CDs on the ZnO NWs.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the PL spectra under excitation of a 325 nm He–Cd laser. The pure CDs film exhibits two emission peaks in the visible range: one for the blue emission at 436 nm and the other for the broad green emission centered at 523 nm. According to the previous reports [32,33], the intrinsic CD core (related to conjugated sp2-domains) and surface states (functional groups) were proved to be responsible for the luminescence of the CDs. For PL of the pure CDs in Fig. 3(a), the 436 and 523 nm peaks of the pure CDs may be attributed to the CD core and the CO surface groups (carbonyl or carboxyl groups), respectively [34–36]. ZnO NWs have a weak near-band-edge (NBE) emission centered at 379 nm and a much stronger deep-level (DL) emission centered at 577 nm in the visible range, indicating that electronic transitions through the DLs are more advantageous than the direct interband transitions in the solution-grown ZnO NWs. The optical property of the CDs/ZnO NWs composite gives three distinct emission peaks: 379, 436, and 533 nm. In comparison with the PL of the pure CDs and ZnO NWs, the 379 and 436 nm emission peaks should be derived from the NBE emission of ZnO and the CDs core, respectively. The broad emission peak at 553 nm seems to be the superposition of emissions from the CDs and ZnO NWs. To evaluate the impact of the PMMA, we also measured the PL property of the PMMA/CDs/ZnO NWs heterojunction. Neither the NBE emission from ZnO NWs at 379 nm nor the blue emission from the CDs at 436 nm is influenced by the PMMA layer because these emission peaks are not changed. As compared with the PL of the CDs/ZnO NWs composite, however, the broad visible emission peak is blue shifted from 533 to 524 nm (which is very consistent with the emission of the pure CDs) due to screen effect of the PMMA layer. These results demonstrate that the thin PMMA layer has not affected the luminescence of the CDs.

Fig. 3(b) shows the I–V characteristics of LED#1, LED#2 and LED#3, all of which exhibit nonlinear rectification characteristics (the forward bias is applied on the Ag electrode unless otherwise stated). In comparison with LED#1, LED#2 has improved rectification characteristic with slightly higher turn-on voltage, which should be attributed to the inferior electron conduction of the CDs. The rectification characteristic is further enhanced and the reverse leakage current is further suppressed after the thin PMMA layer is incorporated (LED#3), which may be the result of an increase in series resistance of the device due to the poor conductivity of the PMMA.

Fig. 4(a), (c), and (e) show the EL spectra of LED#1, LED#2 and LED#3, respectively. The EL intensities from all the LEDs are remarkably boosted as the forward injection current increases. All the EL spectra show a dominantly broad visible band and a much weaker violet band at ~400 nm, indicating that the DL emission from the ZnO NWs is playing a role in the EL. However, in careful comparison with LED#1 that uses pure ZnO NWs, one can find that with inclusion of the CDs, the dominant visible band shows a distinct blue shift from 550 nm of LED#1 to 530 nm of LED#2 and finally 500 nm of LED#3. The blue emission at ~440 nm, which is nearly invisible in LED#1, also emerges dramatically with increasing the forward injection currents after the CDs were added (see the EL of LED#2 and LED#3). All these results suggest that the CDs have played an important role in the EL emission in LED#2 and LED#3.

To further understand the root cause of the EL emission, we separately deconvolute the EL spectra of the LEDs with Gaussian functions, as shown in Fig. 4(b), (d), and (f). For LED#1 based on pure ZnO NWs, we can obtain five sub-bands peaked at 400 nm (Peak 1), 425 nm (Peak 2), 505 nm (Peak 3), 600 nm (Peak 4) and 700 nm (Peak 5) according to the peak-deconvolution results. Each emission sub-band corresponds to a particular recombination process. Peak 1 (violet emission), frequently observed in ZnO, is attributed to the NBE emission of the ZnO NWs. Unlike other results at ~390 nm [37–40], nevertheless, this NBE peak position is slightly red-shifted to the longer wavelength due to the enlargement of ZnO NW grains after annealing. For the blue Peak 2, it is probably originated from the transition from the shallow donor (Zni) to the deep acceptor (VZn) (or the valence band) [41]. Peak 3, the emission in the green range, is related to the oxygen vacancies (VO), especially for solution-produced ZnO NWs [42]. The orange Peak 4 may result from the transitions related to Oi defect levels [43]. Red emissions of ZnO NWs from 620 to 750 nm are often regarded to be associated with VO and Oi, so the red Peak 5 at 700 nm possibly derives from the emissions related to VO and Oi defects in ZnO NWs [44]. For LED#2 and LED#3 including the CDs, two more sub-bands at 460 nm (Peak 6) and 545 nm (Peak 7) were obtained besides those sub-bands in LED#1, as depicted by the dash line in Fig. 4(d) and (f). Obviously, these two sub-bands are distinct from those of the pure ZnO NWs and should be ascribed to the CDs. As the same with PL of the pure CDs, the blue Peak 6 and the green Peak 7 in the EL are related to the inherent structure of the CD core and the CO groups, respectively. It should be noted that these two sub-bands are red-shifted by about 22–24 nm in wavelength in comparison with the PL emission peaks of the pure CDs due to the excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interactions of the CDs on ZnO NWs [23]. Up to date, most of the CDs-based LEDs have only taken advantage of the PL property of the CDs by using CDs as phosphors [45,46]. It is not popular to use CDs as the active emissive layer of a LED owing to the complex emission mechanism of the CDs. However, since we have revealed that the emission bands of 436–460 nm and 523–545 nm are originated from the CDs, designing multicolor LEDs (including WLEDs) by using CDs as the emissive layer shall become more freely.

To evaluate the functions of the CDs and the PMMA layer, we plotted the fitting peak intensity as a function of Peak 1 to 7 and the enhancement factor of each peak for LED#3 versus LED#2 in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(a), addition of the CDs enhances nearly all the sub-bands of the EL of the device and makes the EL involve more spectral information due to the emergence of the blue (Peak 6) and green (Peak 7) emissions from the CDs. After inserting the thin PMMA layer between the CDs and HTL of PEDOT:PSS, all EL intensities of the sub-bands are further enhanced, especially for the blue emission (Peak 2 and Peak 6) and green emission (Peak 3 and Peak 7) (see Fig. 5(a)). Therefore, owing to the greatly improved blue and green emissions from CDs and ZnO NWs as well as their superposition, the emission color of the device obviously tuned from yellowish green toward near white after inserting the PMMA layer, as illustrated by the CIE plots in Fig. 5(c). However, after considering the enhancement factor for each sub-band of LED#3 in Fig. 5(b), we find that the enhancement of the blue emission (Peak 2 and Peak 6) is much stronger than the other sub-bands when the thin PMMA is inserted. Hence, one can conclude that it is the striking enhancement of the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs that plays a dominant role to tune the emission color of the device toward near white, rather than the green or orange emissions.

In addition, the great enhancement of the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs suggests that the thin PMMA layer plays an important role in balancing the injection of the carriers. To explicate the effect of PMMA more clearly, we illustrate the energy band diagram of LED#3 in Fig. 5(d). It is clear that if the PMMA layer is absent, due to the faster mobility and small energy barrier, electrons from the ITO cathode can be easily injected from the CDs/ZnO NWs into the PEDOT:PSS to recombine with the injected holes from the Ag anode, thereby resulting in low-efficiency radiation recombination from CDs or ZnO NWs. However, the situation has become very different after the insertion of a thin PMMA layer. As an insulating layer, the thin PMMA layer can effectively prevent excessive electrons from overflowing into the PEDOT:PSS side energetically, so that the electrons can recombine at the CDs/ZnO NWs side to greatly increase the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs simultaneously.

In summary, we have reported a demonstration of near-white-light LEDs by regulating the blue emission of PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NWs OIHH LEDs with blue emissive CDs. Distinct blue and green bands at 460 (436) and 545 (523) nm in the EL (PL) spectra are found to be originated from the blue CDs. After inserting a thin interfacial modification layer with PMMA between the CDs and HTL of PEDOT:PSS, the EL intensities of all the sub-bands are remarkably boosted (especially for the blue emission that are related to CDs and ZnO NWs) to make the emission color of the device tuned from yellowish green toward near white. Analysis with the energy band diagram reveals that the thin PMMA layer can effectively prevent excessive electrons from overflowing into the PEDOT:PSS energetically, so that the electrons can recombine at the CDs/ZnO NWs side to greatly increase the blue emission from CDs and ZnO NWs. This work paves another feasible way to realize WLEDs by using CDs as the emissive layer and also gives a practical demonstration to regulate the blue emission of ZnO to produce WLEDs.

Chengjun Qi: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yulu Zhou: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xiaoma Tao: Validation, Formal analysis. Hongmei Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yifang Ouyang: Writing - review & editing. Xiaoming Mo: Conceptualization, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11504060, 11405034), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (Grant No. 2018GXNSFBA281163), and Young Teachers' Innovation Training Project of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd. (Grant No. BRP180220).
